title: Raiding the dark transcriptome for a new class of drugs
html_body:
Today in Science and science: A creative stellar collision, NASA’s new administrator on his fast start, and more
Brought to you by From Concept to Reality: Taking Intelligent Agents into the Real World
View this email in your browser
11 March 2026
Today’s Visualized examines the electrifying performance that won this year’s Dance Your Ph.D. competition. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a brilliant stellar merger and what NASA’s new leader had to say about his role.
Drug DEvelopment  |  Science Signaling
Raiding the dark transcriptome for a new class of drugs
Most of the human genome doesn't code for proteins; for decades, much of this so-called noncoding RNA was dismissed as junk. Although research over the past twenty years has shown that many of these molecules actively regulate when and how genes are switched on or off, one major class of these molecules, called long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs), has remained almost entirely on the sidelines of drug development.

A study published this week in Science Signaling demonstrates for the first time that lncRNAs can be synthesized outside cells, engineered into stable therapeutics, and used to tamp down acute inflammation in mice and human immune cells.

Researchers focused on three lncRNAs—GAPLINC, MIST, and DRAIR—previously identified as natural brakes on inflammation. The team built a workflow to synthesize and purify each molecule, applied chemical modifications to improve stability and reduce immune reactivity, and packaged them into lipid nanoparticles to ferry them into cells, similar to the delivery technology used by mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

All three dampened acute inflammation in mouse immune cells and in live mice, each targeting a distinct inflammatory protein: GAPLINC suppressed IL-1β, MIST knocked back TNFα, and DRAIR reduced IL-6. Crucially, a modified form of GAPLINC also reduced inflammation in human immune cells, hinting at clinical potential. Tolerability tests over 8 days showed no signs of organ damage or toxicity.

With more than 35,000 lncRNAs catalogued in the human transcriptome, the vast majority of which have unknown functions, the findings open what the authors describe as an entirely new drug modality. Unlike conventional small molecules or biologics, these molecules are already shaped by evolution to operate inside cells with high specificity, a property the authors say could reduce the risk of side effects compared to existing treatments. “ This is a completely new paradigm for drug discovery,” senior author Omar Khan told Inside Precision Medicine . “The dark transcriptome is a great opportunity to find new treatments that will really change lives in the future.”
Read the paper
Astronomy  |  The Astrophysical Journal Letters
From destruction to creation
An illustration of the galactic merger that caused neutron stars to collide brilliantly.  Maria Cristina Fortuna/NASA/Chandra X-ray Center
The gold in jewelry, the platinum in cars, the iron in blood—all of it came from violent outbursts somewhere in the universe. Researchers have observed one such outburst, a blindingly bright explosion that resulted from two neutron stars colliding and releasing energy.

Astronomers first noticed the collision with the Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope in 2023. Then, using the Chandra X-ray Observatory and the Hubble Space Telescope, they triangulated the explosion to a faint galaxy around 8.5 billion light-years away. Nearby, other galaxies seem to be interacting and crashing into one another, creating huge amounts of dusty debris that can coalesce into stars. The team thinks that the neutron stars in question formed within this debris-filled area around 700 million years ago.

When the stars collided, they didn’t just make a brilliant bang: They formed and scattered heavy elements like gold and iron into the surrounding region. The same kind of crash-bang process likely brought heavy elements to Earth billions of years ago. And it may happen billions of years in the future, when the Milky Way and our neighboring Andromeda galaxy begin to interact and stoke new star formation and collisions.

“We got a rare glimpse into how destruction can be a catalyst for creation,” said author Jane Charlton in a statement.
Read the Paper
POlicy  |  ScienceInsider
NASA’s new administrator on his fast start
Jared Isaacman, NASA’s new administrator, is a man with a plan. Since joining the agency in December, the tech billionaire and pilot has been preoccupied with its workforce, which has been decimated by buyouts and retirements over the past year, and bolstering its human exploration program—including the announcement last month that NASA would accelerate the launch frequency of its costly Moon rocket to more than once a year, in an effort to get astronauts to the Moon’s surface in 2028.

But in an interview, Isaacman was also eager to talk about science , including the robotic missions that make up a hefty portion of the agency’s portfolio. He teased that NASA will soon up its cadence of small robotic missions to the Moon as part of its nascent plans for a long-term human presence, and he’s discussed with President Trump the potential for a new Mars mission in 2028—the next time it’s feasible to launch to the Red Planet.

When asked about his support for studies of global warming, which NASA scientists have long studied as part of its earth science program, Isaacman committed to continuing the agency’s observations, which are crucial for gauging sea level rise, ice sheet melt, and global temperature. But he also lamented what he saw as NASA’s excessive messaging on climate change under past administrations. “You have a previous administration that puts out on NASA letterhead the world is going to end, and then you have the next administration put out on NASA letterhead that this is all a hoax,” he told Science. “How is that useful to anyone right now?”
Read the Q&A
SPONSORED
Wallonia: A story of biopharma growth
What does biopharma growth look like on the ground in Wallonia, Belgium? This Q&A explores how the region has built research capacity, expanded lab space, and supported companies through public investment, clinical networks, and talent development.
Read more
Visualized
This electrifying performance garnered Sofia Papa the top prize in this year’s Dance Your Ph.D. competition.  Sofia Papa/company.mov
 
Sofia Papa started studying physics because she thought the subject “was married with art in a nice way.” Both scientists and artists are driven by curiosity about the world around them, she says, and she dreamt of a future in which these different means of exploring unanswered questions could enrich each other. Now, Papa has found the ultimate way to unite these two passions. The graduate student is the winner of this year’s top prize in Science’s annual Dance Your Ph.D. contest , a competition in which scientists express their research through dance.

In Papa’s winning entry, dancers recreate the piezoelectric effect, the ability of some crystalline materials to generate electricity when subjected to stress. The property is a critical component of technologies such as cigarette lighters and quartz watches. Papa’s dance, full of twists and turns, uses movement as a metaphor for the differences between crystalline and semicrystalline materials, as well as how the charges can become displaced under certain conditions.

“It was engaging all throughout,” says judge Renée Jaworski. She particularly admired Papa’s use of perspective, shadow, and light. Judge Alexa Meade found the dance to be “hypnotic and entrancing,” she said, and driven by the dancers’ ability to move perfectly in sync.
See all the winners
Et Cetera
When the moon hits your eye like a compass in the sky
Traveling by the light of the Moon is tricky. “It’s not in the same place every night, it doesn’t go up or down at the same time, and it’s different in size and brightness,” noted one sensory biologist. Yet bull ants use the Moon to walk in straight lines every night— findings that are “paradigm shifting … I would never have believed this was possible.”
Current Biology Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
A gnawing joy
Ask anyone working in a pet store, and they’ll tell you that rodents like mice and hamsters must have lots of things to gnaw on because their teeth never stop growing. And that’s true—but they don’t do it automatically. They chew compulsively, because it feels good. “When [gnawing] happens, it has this positive reward—this positive feedback—and it keeps going,” one researcher explained. The findings could shed light on compulsive behaviors in other species, including us.
Neuron Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Accurate flatulence
Clinical measurements like urine output can tell a doctor a lot about a patient, but not all the body’s regular functions are currently quantifiable. To get accurate measures of intestinal gas expulsion, a team of researchers designed a quarter-sized sensor that tracks both frequency and volume of hydrogen gas emission over time. “We know what the normal heart rate is, we know what the normal level of cholesterol is, but if you go to the doctor, they don’t know the normal number of farts,” noted one of the researchers behind what they’ve dubbed Smart Underwear. “If you tell them, ‘I’m farting 50 times a day,’ they don’t have really a baseline to compare that to.”
Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X Paper  |  Read more at Science News
AI agents are becoming public actors with reach into the real world, and with real-world consequences … We are the humans who are responsible for the law, ethics, and institutional design, and we are behind the curve.
Undark Viewpoints  |  5 March 2026  |  Adam Schiavi
Last but not least
Apparently bumblebees can breathe underwater. As if I didn’t have enough reasons to be impressed by these busy little pollinators!
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Ana Georgescu, Hannah Richter, Paul Voosen, and Perri Thaler

items.title: lncRNAs can be synthesized outside cells, engineered into stable therapeutics, and used to tamp down acute inflammation
in an interview, Isaacman was also eager to talk about science
the winner of this year’s top prize in Science’s annual Dance Your Ph.D. contest
